target_query: how to handle send me information objection
page_title: What to Say When a Prospect Says "Send Me Information" - Brutus AI
meta_description: When a prospect asks you to send information, they are often choosing a polite exit. Redirect the brush-off before you email a deck into a void.
h1: what to say when a prospect asks you to "send me information"
intro: "send me some information" is the most-rationalized loss in sales. it feels like progress because the prospect did not say no. they did. they just said it in a way that lets them end the call without feeling rude. the rep gets to log a follow-up. the prospect gets to delete the deck later. everyone feels polite. nothing moves. this usually happens after the prospect has heard enough to know they want out, but not enough to justify a hard no. that matters. this is not the same as a cold-call brush-off. it is a conflict-free exit. the job is not to refuse the request. the job is to make the request specific enough that it becomes a real buying step or exposes itself as a brush-off.

example_dialog.speaker: prospect
example_dialog.line: can you just send me some information?

example_dialog.speaker: rep
example_dialog.line: yeah, absolutely. I'll send over our deck.

example_dialog.speaker: rep
example_dialog.line: happy to send it. before I do, what would you actually want me to include that would help you decide if this is worth another conversation?

example_dialog.speaker: prospect
example_dialog.line: pricing and something that shows how this works for a team our size.

psychology: most prospects do not want conflict on a sales call. "send me information" gives them a clean exit that sounds reasonable. the rep accepts because it lets them avoid hearing a harder no. that is the trap. both people choose politeness over clarity. the redirect works because it keeps the request alive but removes the hiding place. now the prospect has to say what would help them decide, or admit there is no decision process.

counter_frames.label: redirect question
counter_frames.copy: ask what they want included that would actually help them decide. vague requests produce vague follow-up.

counter_frames.label: calendar pin
counter_frames.copy: send the material with a small follow-up placeholder. if it is useful, you talk. if not, they decline.

counter_frames.label: specificity test
counter_frames.copy: ask whether they need pricing, a peer example, security detail, or something else. real interest gets specific.

common_mistakes: agreeing immediately because it feels like progress
sending a generic deck with no reason attached
asking what they want you to send but not why they need it
walk_away_when: the prospect has a documented procurement process where info packets are a real evaluation step
you have confirmed you fit their criteria and they gave you a specific decision date
they refuse to define what information would help them decide
brutus_angle: Brutus listens for the transactional pivot: shorter answers, more formal cadence, and the prospect moving from conversation to admin. the live cue is: "don't agree to send. ask what would help them decide."
cta_variant: Brutus flags the second your tone gives the prospect a polite exit. Stop emailing decks into a void. First 5 calls free.
faq_questions: Is sending the deck ever the right move?
How fast should I follow up after sending?
notes: Wave 1 draft payload. Passed JSON, banned-pattern, and rough similarity checks. Do not render to HTML until the gold-standard browser quality pass is accepted.
